title: Input and output
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The same two lines, written three times with a different number.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
hint3.crashed: ReferenceError: withVat is not defined means the function either does not exist or is called something else. The name has to be exactly withVat.
hint2.no-function: The sum is written once and given a name. Then it is called with different numbers.
hint2.still-repeating: Both lines go inside the function, and only once.
why.wrong-function: The function does not give the right answer.
hint2.wrong-function: Twelve with twenty percent tax is 14.4, rounded 14.
hint3.wrong-function: If you get undefined, the function calculates but does not return. The result has to come back out.
hint4.wrong-function: return sends the value back to whoever called the function.
why.wrong-general: The function does not work with an arbitrary number.
hint2.wrong-general: The number should not be written inside. It comes from outside, through the brackets.
hint3.wrong-general: The name in the function's brackets is a blank space. It is filled with whatever you pass when calling it.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.0.text: Three totals with tax. The same two lines, written three times with a different number.
blocks.1.text: Now imagine the tax becomes nineteen percent. Or that there are a hundred totals. Or that somebody asks for rounding down instead.
blocks.3.text: You write the sum once, give it a name, and then call it as many times as you like with different numbers.
blocks.5.text: price in the brackets is a blank space. It is filled with the number you pass when you call it.
blocks.7.text: You will meet the explanation that a function is a piece of code you saved for later. That is true, and useless.
blocks.8.text: A function is something that takes an input and returns an output. Give it 12, get 14. Give it 30, get 36. What it does inside is its own business.
blocks.9.quote: Think of it as a machine with a funnel on top and a spout underneath.
blocks.11.text: Writing a function does not run it. It sits and waits. It runs only when you call it — with its name and brackets.
blocks.13.text: The brackets mean do it now. Without them you get the function itself, unperformed — the same mistake as with string methods in lesson thirteen.
blocks.15.text: Make a function called withVat that takes a price and returns the price with tax, rounded. Exactly that name — the checks look for it.
blocks.16.text: Then call it three times with the numbers 12, 30 and 7. The two lines with the sum should exist in only one place in the whole file.
